# Machine Learning with R: Basic concepts

## Literature links

[Feature engineering](https://feat.engineering/)

[Tidy Modeling with R](https://www.tmwr.org/)

## structured vs. unstructured data

## supervised vs. unsupervised methods

## Resampling methods

-   Creation of new samples based on one observed sample.

-   Helps in creating new synthetic datasets for training machine learning models and to estimate properties of a dataset when the dataset is unknown, difficult to estimate, or when the sample size of the dataset is small.

-   Resampling Methods

    -   Permutation tests (also re-randomization tests)

    -   Bootstrapping

    -   Jackknife (Leave one out validation)

    -   Cross validation

### Permutation tests

-   Permutation reshuffles the observed cases, sampling without replacement, e.g. Random combination of 2 classes (like disease / risk)

-   Original:

| Class 1  | Class 2 |
|----------|---------|
| Affected | Exposed |
| Affected | Exposed |
| Affected | \-      |
| Control  | \-      |
| Control  | \-      |

-   Permutation 1

| Class 1  | Class 2 |
|----------|---------|
| Affected | \-      |
| Affected | Exposed |
| Affected | \-      |
| Control  | \-      |
| Control  | Exposed |

-   repeated x times
-   count, how frequent results as "extreme" as the observation occur by coincidence

```{r}
#| output: false
#| message: false
pacman::p_load(conflicted,wrappedtools,tidyverse, ggbeeswarm,
               broom, boot, Hmisc)

conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter,
                 dplyr::select)
```

Simulation of a weak effect with vaccination reducing cough incidence from 70% to 60%:

```{r}
set.seed(161)
vaccination <- 
  tibble(vaccination = rep(c("no more cough","placebo"),each = 100),
         infection = c(sample(c("yes","no"),size = 100,
                              replace = TRUE,prob = c(.6,.4)),
                       sample(c("yes","no"),size = 100,
                              replace = TRUE,prob = c(.7,.3))))
vaccination |> 
  ggplot(aes(vaccination, fill = infection))+
  geom_bar()+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("lightgreen","salmon"))+
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0,1000,20))
observed_or <- fisher.test(vaccination$vaccination, 
                           vaccination$infection)$estimate
estimated_p <- fisher.test(vaccination$vaccination, 
                           vaccination$infection,
                           simulate.p.value = FALSE)$p.value
extremes_log_or <- c(-log(observed_or), log(observed_or))
n_reps <- 10^3
simulated_or <- tibble(run_i = 1:n_reps,
                       or = NA_real_)
for (shuffle_i in seq_len(n_reps)){
  shuffled_infection <- sample(vaccination$infection)
  simulated_or$or[shuffle_i] <- 
    fisher.test(vaccination$vaccination, 
                shuffled_infection)$estimate
}
ggplot(simulated_or, aes(x = log(or)))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = .1, center = 0,
                 fill = "lightgreen", color = "darkgrey")+
  geom_vline(xintercept = extremes_log_or, color = "salmon")+
  labs(
    title="Distribution of simulated log(ORs) under the Null hypothesis",
    caption = "Vertical lines indicate the observed log(OR) and its negative counterpart")
extremes_count <- 
  table(between(log(simulated_or$or),
                extremes_log_or[1], extremes_log_or[2]))
```

The conventional p-value is `r formatP(estimated_p,ndigits = 5)`, while shuffling the data resulted in `r round(extremes_count[1]/n_reps, 5)`% of the simulated ORs being as extreme as the observed OR.

### Bootstrapping

-   Bootstrapping selects from the population of observed cases, sampling with replacement.

-   Bootstrap is a powerful statistical tool used to quantify the uncertainty of a given model.

```{r}
#| echo: false
boot_data <- tibble(
  SubjectID = factor(1:9),
  x = factor(rep(1:3, times = 3)),
  y = factor(rep(1:3, each = 3)),
  size = rnorm(9, mean = 170, sd = 10),
  fishing = 0)

for (i in 1:9) {
  boot_data <- 
    bind_rows(
      boot_data,
      boot_data |> 
        filter(fishing == 0) |> 
        slice_sample(n = 9, replace = TRUE) |>
        mutate(fishing = i))

}
plot_org <- boot_data |> 
  filter(fishing == 0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y, color = SubjectID)) +
  geom_point(size = 30) +
  geom_label(aes(label = SubjectID), size = 5) +
  scale_x_discrete("", labels = "") +
  scale_y_discrete("", labels = "") +
  labs(title = "Original Data") +
  guides(x = "none", y = "none", color = "none") +
  theme_bw()
plot_org  
plot_boot <- boot_data |> 
  filter(fishing != 0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y, color = SubjectID)) +
  geom_beeswarm(size = 5, alpha = .5, cex = 4) +
  geom_label(aes(label = SubjectID), size = 2) +
  scale_x_discrete("", labels = "") +
  scale_y_discrete("", labels = "") +
  facet_wrap(facets = vars(fishing), ncol = 3, 
             labeller = "label_both") +
  labs(title = "Bootstrap samples") +
  guides(x = "none", y = "none", color = "none") +
  theme_bw()
plot_boot
```

Based on the bootstrap samples, we can visualize variation in outcome **size**:

```{r}
desc_boot <- 
  boot_data |> 
  # filter(fishing != 0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(fishing), y = size)) +
  stat_summary(color = "salmon", fun.data = mean_cl_normal,
               size = 1)+
  geom_beeswarm(alpha = .75, size = 2)+
  ylab("size mean \u00b1 95% CI")+
  scale_x_discrete("bootstrap sample",
                   labels = c("original", paste("boot", 1:9)))
desc_boot
```

<!-- ![](/Graphs/bootstrap_classify.png){fig-align="center"} -->

<!-- ![](/Graphs/Bootstrap_25_0.png){fig-align="center"} -->

A large number of bootstrap (\>1000) samples is usually required to obtain stable results. We simulate 50 cases with true mean of 170 and true SD of 10:

```{r}
n_subjects <- 50
real_mean <- 170
real_sd <- 10
n_boot <- 10^4
set.seed(12345)
experimental_data <- 
  rnorm(n = n_subjects, mean = real_mean, sd = real_sd)
```

Conventional estimates from sample:
```{r}
meansd(experimental_data,roundDig = 4)
meanse(experimental_data,roundDig = 4)
smean.cl.normal(experimental_data)
```

Bootstrap estimates:
```{r}
smean.cl.boot(experimental_data,B = n_boot)
#looped bootstraping
boot_means <- tibble(Mean = NA_real_, .rows = 0)
for (run_i in seq_len(n_boot)){
  boot_means <- 
    add_row(boot_means,
            Mean = mean(sample(x = experimental_data,
                               size = n_subjects,
                               replace = TRUE)))
}

cl_boot <- quantile(boot_means$Mean,probs = c(.025, .975))
ggplot(boot_means, aes(Mean))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = .5, fill = "lightblue", color = "darkgrey")+
  geom_vline(xintercept = cl_boot, linetype = 2)

# package boot
# Custom function to calculate the mean for each resample
boot_mean <- function(d, i) {
  d[i] |> mean()
}
# Sample data
# Execute n_boot bootstrap replicates
boot_results <- boot(
  data = experimental_data, 
  statistic = boot_mean, 
  R = n_boot
)

# View the result
boot_results
# Get multiple types of CIs
boot_ci <- boot.ci(boot_results, type = "basic")

print(boot_ci)
```

### Cross validation

-   Cross-validation is a resampling method used to evaluate the performance of a model on unseen data.

-   It involves partitioning the data into `k` subsets, training the model on one subset (training set) made of k-1 subsets, and evaluating it on the left-out subset (testing set).

-   This process is repeated `k` times, with each subset serving as the testing set once, and the results are averaged to provide an overall performance estimate.

-   Cross-validation is often repeated multiple times to reduce the variance of the performance estimates and to get a more reliable estimate of the model's performance. 10 repeated 5-fold cross-validations would provide 50 estimates of the model's performance, capturing average as well as possible extreme results.

![](/Graphs/8-Stratified-K-Fold-Cross-Validation.jpg){fig-align="center"}

\newpage

### Jacknife

-   Jackknife (or **L**eave **O**ne **O**ut) is resampling without replacement, where one observation is left out at a time to create new samples. It is used to estimate the bias and variance of a statistic, as well as to construct confidence intervals.

-   Creates n samples of size n-1

<!-- ![](/Graphs/jackknife_estimate.jpg){fig-align="center"} -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
loo_data <- tibble(
  SubjectID = factor(1:9),
  x = factor(rep(1:3, times = 3)),
  y = factor(rep(1:3, each = 3)),
  drop = 0)

for (i in 1:9) {
  loo_data <- 
    bind_rows(
      loo_data,
      loo_data |> 
        filter(drop == 0,
               SubjectID != i) |> 
        mutate(drop = i))

}
plot_org <- loo_data |> 
  filter(drop == 0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y, color = SubjectID)) +
  geom_point(size = 30) +
  geom_label(aes(label = SubjectID), size = 5) +
  scale_x_discrete("", labels = "") +
  scale_y_discrete("", labels = "") +
  labs(title = "Original Data") +
  guides(x = "none", y = "none", color = "none") +
  theme_bw()
plot_org  
plot_loo <- loo_data |> 
  filter(drop != 0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y, color = SubjectID)) +
  geom_point(size = 5) +
  geom_label(aes(label = SubjectID), size = 2) +
  scale_x_discrete("", labels = "") +
  scale_y_discrete("", labels = "") +
  facet_wrap(facets = vars(drop), ncol = 3, 
             labeller = "label_both") +
  labs(title = "LOO samples") +
  guides(x = "none", y = "none", color = "none") +
  theme_bw()
plot_loo
```
